title: Field Operations
text:
Construct a field with three elements. In other words, define field addition + and
field multiplication . such that the set F = {0, 1, a} becomes a field.

title: Field Operations
text:
We are given a set F = {0, 1, a}, and we have to define the field addition ’+’, and the field
multiplication ’.’ such that F becomes a field.
Recall that each operation must: be closed, be associative, be commutative, have an iden-
tity element in the field, and each element (except the 0 for the ’.’ operation) has an
inverse. They must be distributive as well. Let’s consider each operation alone first. Let
’0’ be the field’s zero, and ’*’ be a placeholder, and x, y, z, and u are variables in F, then:
′+′
0
1

∗
0 + x = x ∀x ∈F
−−−−−−−−−→
′+′

u
z
i. 1 + a = y, and a + 1 = u, but the field addition must be commutative, therefore
a + 1 = 1 + a = y, then u = y. We now have to get the values of x, y, and z.
ii. Neither x nor y can equal 1 because otherwise, 1 + 1 = x = 1, add the additive inverse
of 1 from the left,(−1 + 1) + 1 = −1 + 1. LHS = 0 + 1 = 1, and RHS = 0. Similarly,
y ̸= 1, y ̸= a, and z ̸= a.
iii. Moreover, x ̸= y, otherwise 1 + 1 = 1 + a →1 = a. Similarly, z ̸= y.

iv. Since y /∈{1, a}, then y = 0. Since x ̸= 1 and x ̸= y, then x = a. Similarly, z = 1, i.e.,
1 and a are additive inverses over F.
The addition operation input/output table is then:
′+′

Now, we only have to decide x. The element a must have a multiplicative inverse, i.e., a
’1’ should appear on its row (or column), then x = 1, and we have:
′.′

By construction, both operations are closed, commutative (treat the table as a matrix
A and notice that A = A⊤), have an identity, and each element in F, except ’0’ in the
case of ’.’, has its inverse under the operation. Note that this is the only solution that
satisfies the four aforementioned conditions, if they turned out to be non-associative or
non-distributive, then this set can never be a field.
Associativity of ’+’
To verify associativity of ’+’, we need to verify that ∀x, y, z ∈
F
(x + y) + z = x + (y + z). To do this exhaustively, we will have to go through 33 = 27
combinations, but we can think carefully and leverage the other verified properties to
minimize the verification effort:
i. If any of them is = 0, then the property is satisfied for all combinations of the other
two.
ii. We restrict our attention to x, y, z ∈{1, a}.
So we now have to consider only 8
combinations. if x = y = z, then the property is satisfied. Then we don’t have to
consider the cases where x = y = z = a or x = y = z = 1.
iii. You can easily show that the remaining 6 combinations satisfy the property.
Therefore, ’+’ is associative.
Associativity of ’.’
Is (x.y).z = x.(y.z) ∀x, y, z ∈F? We can reduce the number of
verifications by noticing that if any variable is = 0, both sides = 0. If any variable = 1,
then the property holds for all the other values of the other two variables. We are only left
with the case of x = y = z = a, where the property is satisfied. Then ’.’ is associative.

Distributivity
Is x.(y + z) = x.y + x.z ∀x, y, z ∈F?
i. If x = 0: LHS = 0.(y + z) = 0, RHS = 0.y + 0.z = 0 + 0 = 0, thus the property is
satisfied for any values of y, z.
ii. If x = 1: LHS = 1.(y + z) = y + z, RHS = 1.y + 1.z = y + z, thus the property is
satisfied for any values of y, z.
iii. If x = a: If any of y or z = 0, the property is satisfied for all the values of the
other one: x.(0 + z) = x.z, and x.0 + x.z = 0 + x.z = x.z. x.(y + 0) = x.y, and
x.y + x.0 = x.y + 0 = x.u. We are only left with four cases (3 only if you notice that
by commutativity x.(y + z) = x.(z + y), and x.y + x.z = x.z + x.y).
A. a.(1 + 1) = a.a = 1, and a.1 + a.1 = a + a = 1.
B. a.(a + a) = a.1 = a, and a.a + a.a = 1 + 1 = a.
C. a.(1 + a) = a.0 = 0, and a.1 + a.a = a + 0.
Then distributivity is satisfied. Therefore F is a field equipped with ’+’ and ’.’ as defined
above.
Note
Let a = 2, then the addition becomes the real numbers addition modulo 3 and
the multiplication becomes the real multiplication module 3. This is the Galois field of 3
(GF(3)).

multiplication ’.’ such that F becomes a field.
Recall that each operation must: be closed, be associative, be commutative, have an iden-
tity element in the field, and each element (except the 0 for the ’.’ operation) has an
inverse. They must be distributive as well. Let’s consider each operation alone first. Let

By construction, both operations are closed, commutative (treat the table as a matrix

case of ’.’, has its inverse under the operation. Note that this is the only solution that
satisfies the four aforementioned conditions, if they turned out to be non-associative or
non-distributive, then this set can never be a field.
Associativity of ’+’
To verify associativity of ’+’, we need to verify that ∀x, y, z ∈
F
(x + y) + z = x + (y + z). To do this exhaustively, we will have to go through 33 = 27
combinations, but we can think carefully and leverage the other verified properties to
minimize the verification effort:
i. If any of them is = 0, then the property is satisfied for all combinations of the other
two.
ii. We restrict our attention to x, y, z ∈{1, a}.
So we now have to consider only 8
combinations. if x = y = z, then the property is satisfied. Then we don’t have to
consider the cases where x = y = z = a or x = y = z = 1.
iii. You can easily show that the remaining 6 combinations satisfy the property.
Therefore, ’+’ is associative.
Associativity of ’.’
Is (x.y).z = x.(y.z) ∀x, y, z ∈F? We can reduce the number of
verifications by noticing that if any variable is = 0, both sides = 0. If any variable = 1,
then the property holds for all the other values of the other two variables. We are only left
with the case of x = y = z = a, where the property is satisfied. Then ’.’ is associative.

Distributivity
Is x.(y + z) = x.y + x.z ∀x, y, z ∈F?
i. If x = 0: LHS = 0.(y + z) = 0, RHS = 0.y + 0.z = 0 + 0 = 0, thus the property is
satisfied for any values of y, z.
ii. If x = 1: LHS = 1.(y + z) = y + z, RHS = 1.y + 1.z = y + z, thus the property is
satisfied for any values of y, z.
iii. If x = a: If any of y or z = 0, the property is satisfied for all the values of the
other one: x.(0 + z) = x.z, and x.0 + x.z = 0 + x.z = x.z. x.(y + 0) = x.y, and
x.y + x.0 = x.y + 0 = x.u. We are only left with four cases (3 only if you notice that
by commutativity x.(y + z) = x.(z + y), and x.y + x.z = x.z + x.y).
A. a.(1 + 1) = a.a = 1, and a.1 + a.1 = a + a = 1.
B. a.(a + a) = a.1 = a, and a.a + a.a = 1 + 1 = a.
C. a.(1 + a) = a.0 = 0, and a.1 + a.a = a + 0.
Then distributivity is satisfied. Therefore F is a field equipped with ’+’ and ’.’ as defined
above.
Note
Let a = 2, then the addition becomes the real numbers addition modulo 3 and
the multiplication becomes the real multiplication module 3. This is the Galois field of 3
(GF(3)).

title: Field Operations
text:
Using the results from part (a), solve for x ∈F such that
i. a.x + 1 = a.
ii. a.x.x + 1 = a.

title: Field Operations
text:
Now, Let’s solve for x ∈F such that:
i. a.x + 1 = a: This is analogous to linear equations over R (try graphing it). Since
this is a finite field, we can solve this using two methods, namely, exhaustive search,
where ewe plug in values for x and check if the equality is satisfied, or deal with it
algebraically as we used to do in high school. We will opt here for the second method.
Add the additive inverse of 1, namely a, from the right:
(a.x + 1) + a = a + a
LHS = a.x + (1 + a)
(Associativity of ’+’)
= a.x + 0 = a.x
RHS = a + a = 1
∴a.x = 1
Then x is the multiplicative inverse of a, namely x = a (multiply by the multiplicative
inverse of a from the left and apply associativity).
ii. a.x.x + 1 = a: The first step is similar to the previous equation, we add the aditive
inverse of 1 form the right and use associativity, then a.x.x = 1. Now we multiply
with the multiplicative inverse of a from the left and apply associativity. Therefore
x.x = a. The values for x.x are on the diagonal of the ’.’ table. Obviously no element
in F when multiplied by itself becomes = a. There is no solution for x ∈F.
Recall that x2 = −1 has no solution in R, although it has two solutions in C, namely
± j.

title: Field Operations
text:
Problem 2(b):
Using the results from part (a), solve for x ∈F such that
i. a.x + 1 = a.
ii. a.x.x + 1 = a.

Solution:
Now, Let’s solve for x ∈F such that:
i. a.x + 1 = a: This is analogous to linear equations over R (try graphing it). Since
this is a finite field, we can solve this using two methods, namely, exhaustive search,
where ewe plug in values for x and check if the equality is satisfied, or deal with it
algebraically as we used to do in high school. We will opt here for the second method.
Add the additive inverse of 1, namely a, from the right:
(a.x + 1) + a = a + a
LHS = a.x + (1 + a)
(Associativity of ’+’)
= a.x + 0 = a.x
RHS = a + a = 1
∴a.x = 1
Then x is the multiplicative inverse of a, namely x = a (multiply by the multiplicative
inverse of a from the left and apply associativity).
ii. a.x.x + 1 = a: The first step is similar to the previous equation, we add the aditive
inverse of 1 form the right and use associativity, then a.x.x = 1. Now we multiply
with the multiplicative inverse of a from the left and apply associativity. Therefore
x.x = a. The values for x.x are on the diagonal of the ’.’ table. Obviously no element
in F when multiplied by itself becomes = a. There is no solution for x ∈F.
Recall that x2 = −1 has no solution in R, although it has two solutions in C, namely
± j.

Problem 3(a):
{x | xi ≥0}

Solution:
No, it is not a subspace since the set is not closed under scalar multiplication. For example,
suppose x ∈S = {x | xi ≥0} and α = −1. Then

The set S = {x | x1 = 0} is a subspace. Suppose x1, x2 ∈S. Then
y = x1 + x2;
y1 = 0 =⇒y ∈S
and let α ∈R
z = αx1;
z1 = 0 =⇒z ∈S
Since the set is closed under addition and scalar multiplication, it is a subset of Rn.

The set S = {x | x1x2 = 0} is not a subspace since it is not closed under addition. To see
this let e1 = [1, 0, 0, . . . , 0] ∈S and e2 = [0, 1, 0, . . . , 0] ∈S. But
e1 + e2 /∈S

Problem 3(c):
{x | x1x2 = 0}

Solution:
The set S = {x | x1x2 = 0} is not a subspace since it is not closed under addition. To see
this let e1 = [1, 0, 0, . . . , 0] ∈S and e2 = [0, 1, 0, . . . , 0] ∈S. But
e1 + e2 /∈S
{x | Ax = b where b ̸= 0}

The set S = {x | Ax = b where b ̸= 0} is not a subspace since it is not closed under
addition. Suppose x1, x2 ∈S. Then
A(x1 + x2) = Ax1 + Ax2 = 2b ̸= b
Note that, this set is also not closed under scalar multiplication.

Problem 3(d):
{x | Ax = b where b ̸= 0}

Solution:
The set S = {x | Ax = b where b ̸= 0} is not a subspace since it is not closed under
addition. Suppose x1, x2 ∈S. Then
A(x1 + x2) = Ax1 + Ax2 = 2b ̸= b
Note that, this set is also not closed under scalar multiplication.

The set = {[x1, x2, x3, x4] ∈R4 |x3 = x1 + x2, x4 = x1 −x2} is a subspace. First not that
this set is non-empty and [0, 0, 0, 0] ∈S. Let x, y ∈S, then
z = x + y = [x1 + y1, x2 + y2, x3 + y3, x4 + y4]
we have
z3 = x3 + y3 = (x1 + x2) + (y1 + y2) = (x1 + y1) + (x2 + y2) = z1 + z2
z4 = x4 + y4 = (x1 −x2) + (y1 −y2) = (x1 + y1) −(x2 + y2) = z1 −z2
Thus S is closed under vector addition. Let x ∈S, and α ∈R, then
y = αx = [αx1, αx2, αx3, αx4]
we have,
y3 = αx3 = α(x1 + x2) = αx1 + αx2 = y1 + y2
y4 = αx4 = α(x1 −x2) = αx1 −αx2 = y1 −y2
Hence S is also closed under scalar multiplication and it is a subspace.

Problem 3(e):
{[x1, x2, x3, x4] ∈R4 | x3 = x1 + x2, x4 = x1 −x2}

Solution:
The set = {[x1, x2, x3, x4] ∈R4 |x3 = x1 + x2, x4 = x1 −x2} is a subspace. First not that
this set is non-empty and [0, 0, 0, 0] ∈S. Let x, y ∈S, then
z = x + y = [x1 + y1, x2 + y2, x3 + y3, x4 + y4]
we have
z3 = x3 + y3 = (x1 + x2) + (y1 + y2) = (x1 + y1) + (x2 + y2) = z1 + z2
z4 = x4 + y4 = (x1 −x2) + (y1 −y2) = (x1 + y1) −(x2 + y2) = z1 −z2
Thus S is closed under vector addition. Let x ∈S, and α ∈R, then
y = αx = [αx1, αx2, αx3, αx4]
we have,
y3 = αx3 = α(x1 + x2) = αx1 + αx2 = y1 + y2
y4 = αx4 = α(x1 −x2) = αx1 −αx2 = y1 −y2
Hence S is also closed under scalar multiplication and it is a subspace.

All matrices that commute with a given matrix A ∈Rm×m

The set S = {B ∈Rn×n | AB = BA} is a subspace. First note that the set is non-empty
as A ∈S. Let B1, B2 ∈S, Then
A(B1 + B2) = AB1 + AB2 = B1A + B2A = (B1 + B2)A
hence (B1 + B2) ∈S and let α ∈R
A(αB1) = αAB1 = αB1A = (αB1)A
hence αB1 ∈S. Since S is closed under addition and scalar multiplication, it is a subspace.

Problem 3(h):
All matrices that commute with a given matrix A ∈Rm×m

Solution:
The set S = {B ∈Rn×n | AB = BA} is a subspace. First note that the set is non-empty
as A ∈S. Let B1, B2 ∈S, Then
A(B1 + B2) = AB1 + AB2 = B1A + B2A = (B1 + B2)A
hence (B1 + B2) ∈S and let α ∈R
A(αB1) = αAB1 = αB1A = (αB1)A
hence αB1 ∈S. Since S is closed under addition and scalar multiplication, it is a subspace.
{X ∈Rn×n | X2 = X}

No the set S = {X ∈Rn×n | X2 = X} is not a subspace. To see this, simply let X = I ∈
S, α = 2. Then
(αX)2 = α2X2 = α2X ̸= X
Since the set is not closed under scalar multiplication, it is not a subspace. This set is also
not closed under addition.

Problem 3(i):
{X ∈Rn×n | X2 = X}

Solution:
No the set S = {X ∈Rn×n | X2 = X} is not a subspace. To see this, simply let X = I ∈
S, α = 2. Then
(αX)2 = α2X2 = α2X ̸= X
Since the set is not closed under scalar multiplication, it is not a subspace. This set is also
not closed under addition.
{X ∈Rn×n | trace(X) = 0}

Yes the set S = {X ∈Rn×n | trace(X) = 0} is a subspace. Let B1, B2 ∈S, Then
trace(B1 + B2) = trace(B1) + trace(B2) = 0
and α ∈R
trace(αB1) = α trace(B1) = 0
Since the set is closed under addition and scalar multiplication; it is a subspace.

Problem 3(j):
{X ∈Rn×n | trace(X) = 0}

Solution:
Yes the set S = {X ∈Rn×n | trace(X) = 0} is a subspace. Let B1, B2 ∈S, Then
trace(B1 + B2) = trace(B1) + trace(B2) = 0
and α ∈R
trace(αB1) = α trace(B1) = 0
Since the set is closed under addition and scalar multiplication; it is a subspace.
Show that Pn(R) is a vector space. What is the dimension of this vector space?

We show that the 10 vector space axioms hold. In the following, denote f1(x) = Pn
k=0 akxk, f2(x) =
Pn
k=0 bkxk, f3(x) = Pn
k=0 ckxk.ak, bk, ck, α, β, γ ∈R. f1(x), f2(x), f3(x) ∈Pn(R).
A1. For f1(x), f2(x): f1(x)+f2(x) = Pn
k=0(ak+bk)xk, ak+bk ∈R →f1(x)+f2(x) ∈Pn(R).
A2. For f1(x), f2(x), f3(x): (f1(x) + f2(x)) + f3(x) = Pn
k=0(ak + bk + ck)xk = Pn
k=0 akxk +

Is the union ∪m
n=1Pn a vector space? Does this contradict or comply with something
you learned in class?

The union Sm
n=1 Pn is a vector space.
Consider f(x) = Pn
i=0 αixi ∈Pn. So for n ≤m, f(x) = Pn
i=0 αixi + Pm
i=n+1 0 · xi ∈Pm.
Thus for any f(x) ∈Pn, we see that also f(x) ∈Pn, and so we can infer that Pn ⊆Pm.
Now, in class we learned that a union of subspaces is a subspace, only when one of the
subspaces is contained in another. Thus, using this, we can say that Sm
n=1 Pn is a subspace
(and thus a self-contained vector space).

Find a basis for P4 containing {x2 + 1, x2 −1}

The standard basis for P4 is given by {1, x, x2, x3, x4}. Notice that x2 = 1
2((x2 + 1) + (x2 −
1)), 1 = 1
2((x2 + 1) −(x2 −1)). It is clear that the rest of the standard basis is independent
of the set elements as they are polynomials of different degrees, thus a possible basis would
be {x, x2 + 1, x2 −1, x3, x4}.

Problem 4(c):
Find a basis for P4 containing {x2 + 1, x2 −1}

Solution:
The standard basis for P4 is given by {1, x, x2, x3, x4}. Notice that x2 = 1
2((x2 + 1) + (x2 −
1)), 1 = 1
2((x2 + 1) −(x2 −1)). It is clear that the rest of the standard basis is independent
of the set elements as they are polynomials of different degrees, thus a possible basis would
be {x, x2 + 1, x2 −1, x3, x4}.
Find a basis for P2 from the set {1 + x, x + x2, x + 2x2, 2x + 3x2, 1 + 2x + x2}

Consider the set 1 + x, x + x2, 2x + 3x2.
Consider a(1 + x) + b(x + x2) + c(2x + 3x2) = 0
Rewriting,(a)1 + (a + b + 2c)x + (b + 3c)x2 = 0
Hence a = 0, (a + b + 2c) = 0, and (b + 3c) = 0
Solving the equations, a = b = c = 0. Hence the 3 elements from the set are linearly
independent. Now we know that a basis for P2 will contain 3 elements, hence a set of 3
linearly independent elements from P2 will make a basis. Hence the set 1+x, x+x2, 2x+3x2
is a basis.

Problem 4(d):
Find a basis for P2 from the set {1 + x, x + x2, x + 2x2, 2x + 3x2, 1 + 2x + x2}

Solution:
Consider the set 1 + x, x + x2, 2x + 3x2.
Consider a(1 + x) + b(x + x2) + c(2x + 3x2) = 0
Rewriting,(a)1 + (a + b + 2c)x + (b + 3c)x2 = 0
Hence a = 0, (a + b + 2c) = 0, and (b + 3c) = 0
Solving the equations, a = b = c = 0. Hence the 3 elements from the set are linearly
independent. Now we know that a basis for P2 will contain 3 elements, hence a set of 3
linearly independent elements from P2 will make a basis. Hence the set 1+x, x+x2, 2x+3x2
is a basis.
The set of all n × n real-valued symmetric matrices over R.

Consider A1, A2 ∈S, so (A1 + A2)T = AT
1 + AT
2 = (A1 + A2) ∈S.
Consider A1 ∈S, α ∈R, so (αA1)T = αA1
T = αA1 ∈S.
Thus, the set is closed under addition and scalar multiplication so it is a subspace of the
vector space Rn×n over R and hence a vector space.

The set of all n × n complex-valued symmetric matrices over C.

S is a vector space and the proof is identical to section (a), but now over the field C instead
of R.

Problem 5(b):
The set of all n × n complex-valued symmetric matrices over C.

Solution:
S is a vector space and the proof is identical to section (a), but now over the field C instead
of R.
The set of all n × n complex-valued Hermitian matrices over R.

S is a vector space and the proof is identical to section (a), but now using the Hermitian
operator instead of transpose. Note that since we are still working over the field of real
numbers, proving the scalar multiplication is given by:
A1 ∈S, α ∈R, so (αA1)H = αHA1
H = αA1 ∈S since αH = α as it is a real number, so
its complex conjugate is equal to itself.

Problem 5(c):
The set of all n × n complex-valued Hermitian matrices over R.

Solution:
S is a vector space and the proof is identical to section (a), but now using the Hermitian
operator instead of transpose. Note that since we are still working over the field of real
numbers, proving the scalar multiplication is given by:
A1 ∈S, α ∈R, so (αA1)H = αHA1
H = αA1 ∈S since αH = α as it is a real number, so
its complex conjugate is equal to itself.
